transcript.text: Great to meet you.
How's it going?
Nice to meet you, too.
Can you see me and hear me all right?
So far, so good, yeah.
Okay, that's good.
Thanks.
I see we have a couple of note-takers here, but I guess it's the two of us, right?
Yeah, just the two of us.
So, yeah, a bit of like a...
Because you came into our process a little bit different than what we're used to, and this was passed to Marcel's EA.
kind of just ran with it and scheduled with Andy and myself.
That's not typically how we would handle this.
so like Andy and I, when we go through this process, we have very different things we go through.
That would be quite ambitious to do in 30 minutes.
And so my portion typically is to go through strategy and some specific examples of how you've driven impact at different brands you've worked with.
But Andy will, you know, focus a bit more on kind of the operational side, digging your...

Background a little bit more deeply, but, so yeah, you came into us as a little bit of a different approach, you know, I remember Marcel bringing up your name in the past, so recall, you know, I'd have a little bit of context, but just quick on me, I've been at GrowthX for a while now, just past my year mark here, I was also the first customer, so I've gotten to see all sorts of different angles of all this craziness that has grown, what we've grown to today.

I was initially leading the delivery side of the org and onboarded a lot of our customers and a lot of our team, and then we were, you know, had a need to get Marcel out of the day-to-day of sales, so spent some time on that side of the house for a few months.
Now we have a founding AE who's crushing it, and that's given me the opportunity to jump back on the delivery side again, and so, I'm sure.
In a couple months' time, my role will probably be different, but, you know, just going wherever I can to be helpful.
Previous to this, I have a long background in digital and growth.

I spent 20 years in the space agency side and in-house, and a lot of brands would just hire me because typically they're trying to diversify their channels, be less dependent on paid search and figure out where they can grow, and that became kind of the theme for a lot of my career and why brands would hire me, and part of what eventually led me to hiring GrowthX to help me out with some of those problems.
And so, anyways, that's a little bit about me.
But before I jump into some of the particular questions I have on my end, would love just a very quick overview on your side.
Sure, thank you.
So, the short version is that, you know, I finished with my military service, and then, you know, spent four years practically wandering around, trying to, you know, figure things out.
Then it's...
some point, I started, I got on Upwork and started, you know, bidding on jobs there for as low as, you know, $3 per hour.
I did that for a while.
And then in the meantime, got a couple of clients, worked as a freelancer for a couple of years.
And at some point, I decided to start an agency, which was back in 2020 when I launched Minutia.
So I launched that, took that to a very nice level.
At the top, we were, I think, 19 to 20 full-time people plus contractors working with us, you know, for all sorts of tasks.
And I would say that, you know, at some point last year, or a bit, you know, before that, I, I would say that I just lost interest in the business.
And that I was looking for other things to do.
I started another agency with a friend of mine as a co-founder earlier this year, which was also when I also put, you know, the conversation with Marcel on pause.
And I told him, in fact, that I know that I'm most likely making a mistake, but I feel that I need to do that.
And so I did it.
We loved the idea.
I just, you know, it didn't take off as we expected.
And so we are winding it down by the end of the year.
And I'm at a stage where I need a change and a new challenge.
And thus, I told Marcel that you're the first person I'm, you know, signing to.
And I guess this is, you know, how we ended up on this call.
Very cool.
Appreciate the context.
What, what, what, what, what, what,
So what was it about this space as you went through that journey that really drew you to building Minutia and going that route?
What pulled you into that?
Look, I'm a con person.
I like content, right?
For better or worse, I like it.
And it was also the thing that I think I was good at.
And so that's how it started.
Then moving on, we added like SEO capabilities and whatnot.
We started like diving a bit deeper into things.
So it wasn't just content, but it was driven by content to a large extent.
And so it's just something that I happened to like and I was also good at.
That was the pulling factor, I guess, in this whole journey.
Yeah.
Cool.
Helpful.
Well, appreciate the context.
A few different things that I just want to kind of go through today.

You know, the theme again is going to be around just like how you think about strategy and would love to go through very particular examples, you know, how you think about kind of balancing short and long term results for any customers that you've worked with and things in that general theme.
I'll make sure I also give you some time for questions at the end, but just jumping right into that.
So we'd love to just like start with a particular example that comes to mind, you know, where, whether it is most notable impact, maybe fulfilling impact, whatever that may be from your perspective.

Would love to just first start with that, get a bit of an overview of a particular case that comes to mind and would love to just understand kind of how you went through from the phase of identifying opportunities that drove that impact to how you thought about it.
And have some follow-ups.
Yeah.
So you mentioned the word fulfilling.
And so I will pick that word and I will share an example based on that because I've driven, you know, a lot of like traffic for and impact for SaaS websites and whatnot.
But the word fulfilling, I think, is key here.
Back in COVID, I think it was around August, we got contacted by a company in the promotional product space.
And the website was like, at least to my eyes as a tech guy, was, you know, kind of a disgrace.
But I was like, okay, I will get on that call.
And we got on that call and like I saw on the other end, a person who needed help because their business, his business was crushed by COVID.
And so what we did is that, you know, we slowly started identifying opportunities.
Which back then, that was before the intent switched and you started seeing more e-commerce pages on these search.
It was mostly listicles, right?
So for example, it could be something like corporate gifts, right?
And so on.
And it would be mostly listicles.
And so we started doing this listicle after listicle and so on and so forth and building the authority of the website.
And that, at some point, obviously, down the line, started picking up and became the number one driver of leads for this business.
And this was a one-month sale, right?
And we would compete head-to-head with brands like Swag.com and SwagUp and whatnot.
And we would actually, in fact, beat them, right?
And most importantly, you know, turn things around for a business that was crashed due to COVID.
The company, by the way, is SwagDrop, right?
SwagDrop.com.
Then we also suggested at some point that they rebrand to SwagDrop and, you know, buy the .com domain and, like, redirect everything, which definitely helped.

And, you know, when you say the word fulfilling, I would say that was one of the, like, most fulfilling examples of helping and seeing the impact that, you know, building a channel from scratch can have to, like, a person's life.
And, you know, ability to work and have, like, work and money to live with, right?
So that's one thing that comes to mind.
Cool.
So as you shaped that strategy, can you walk me through a little bit of, you mentioned how you were thinking about the SERP and where to play.
Can you give me a bit more context of how you all, like, built and ultimately.
You gave me a few of the tactics.
I would love to understand a little bit of the key hypotheses you had for the strategy and how that informed what you developed to drive those results.
Yeah.
So the thing is that for this particular example, when people search for promotional products, we have, in most cases, two distinct categories.
You have those who are searching for, oh, I need, you know, five T-shirts because we have this, like, event that we would like to give them to.
And you have these other use cases that are, we have a rebrand for 1,200 employees and we want to ship them, you know, to ship packages worldwide, which is obviously what you need, right?
Like, the kinds of leads you want to take.
And so early on, we saw that some of the terms we would target would, like, drive traffic from category A, whereas our client wanted, you know, traffic from, and leads from category B, right?
And so we would...
Try to dig deeper there and see whether we can uncover more opportunities from that category, right?
And this, as I mentioned, like I mentioned one use case, like anything that's rebranding related, right, could fall under that category.
But that's, of course, like once you get in and you kind of understand the nuances of the business and you also get feedback from the client.

I would say that at the beginning, especially if we are talking about like a website that's a blank canvas, which I don't think is the case with most of GrowthX's clients, you need to build topical authority.
So you need to do like some topics, either you like it or not, right?
And that's regardless of like, you know, is it a Google search world or is it a Google search plus AI search world, right?
And so certain things need to be done, but then, you know, you get the feedback and you see the analytics, you see where like business comes.
And you will just, you know, have to dig deeper into these topics and, like, these use cases and so on.
And ideally support some of these pages and, like, pillars or, you know, whatever it is that you're building with some, like, brand mentions and links and so on and so on.
Makes sense.
Now that you have all this context going through some of those, you know, iterations of your strategy, what would you say is something you'd do differently now looking back at it?
Maybe something that didn't quite land that you had high conviction in, whatever it may be.
In this particular example, it was my suggestion because we saw that one of the target audiences was HR folks, right?
They would come in and, like, they would, I don't know why, but they weren't the only ones, but they were one of the audiences that would, you know, like, ask for help with swag.
And I was like, okay, if that's the case, why don't we do an HR glossary?
Which...
Retrospect was a big mistake because the website then started being associated with HR, which was not the case.
And that was a mistake that we shouldn't have done.

It's okay doing some topics like around, even for this particular example, you know, if we assume that we want HR folks to get on the website, you know, write something like Bamboo HR alternatives or whatnot, and then retarget them ideally because that by itself is not enough.
But going as far as creating a glossary was, in retrospect, a mistake.
And yeah, we shouldn't have done it.
And this, again, this recommendation came from me.
So I take full ownership for this oversight.
Yeah.
So like topically, it just got like created mixed signals.
Diluted.
Like what is this website actually like focusing in and all?
Yeah, yeah, Exactly.
Yeah.
Interesting.
Uh.
How about, Ed, do you have any, like, AEO-specific examples that come to mind as well?
Yes, sure.
So at Minutia, we were working with a company called MailSearch, and they are technical SaaS.
They have, you know, like, quite a few thousand stars on GitHub and, like, hundreds of paying customers and whatnot.
Now, the important thing about them, which is one of the main things that people are missing about AEO, is that they have a very strong GitHub repo, right?
And this is all, you know, consumed by LLMs.
So LLMs, in this case, have a lot of context about MailSearch, what the product is, what its capabilities are, and so on and so forth.
And that's what we'd see, is that, what we saw is that, like, you know, we would publish a page, and, like, on Google Search, just a few hours in some cases, the page would run.
Very high, with some optimization, you know, using ClearScope and all that good stuff.
Good old stuff, though, because it's not new, but, and the same thing would apply to LLMs.
Like, many of the pages would be picked up there.

Now, LLMs and, you know, all this space of AI search, as I see it, is, has been kind of used as a vehicle by the SEO community because they needed a shelter, you know, because their world was shaken by the volatilities in search.
But in all honesty, I don't think it's anything beyond just, you know, brand, great product, content, and, okay, you know, unbiased mentions and, like, a good overall reputation online, right?
So you may, it sounds like you may live in the camp that, you know, I've seen this as been a topic of discussion recently in the, in the, the, the link.
I think I just think that it's not just, even though you can obviously hack your way into the LLMs, it's not that difficult, right?
Like you do all these listicles and you publish them on different websites and whatnot.
not assuming the client is okay and there are no, you know, brand kind of legal kind of, you know, implications there.
But it's not rocket science.
It can manipulate it at this point, at least.
But it also goes beyond just, you know, SEO, right?
Because you need to have the brand, you need to have great product.
And as I said, and a study was also published that kind of confirms that, it seems that, like, products that have more reviews on websites like G2.
A sites tend to perform better on LLMs, right?
Which makes sense because it's part of this online reputation work, right?
So I think that's that.
And now if a brand doesn't have that, you obviously need to build that in a somewhat sneaky way.

But if the client is willing to also run campaigns to get G2 reviews and so on and improve their reputation there, this can always help towards like, you know, accelerating things and getting these results faster.
Yeah, makes sense.
Cool.
As you're thinking about building a strategy, say, for a new customer, how do you think about balancing the short and the long term when you're developing your strategy?
What would be your process for going through that?
The thing is that if we are talking about a company that has, or let's say a website that has a content inventory, inventory.
Sure, content program, in other words, your starting point should obviously be, you know, updates, right?
Since they have something there, you need to start from content updates and then see all the gaps that they may have and build new content, right?

If that's not the case and it's, you know, a blank canvas, I would say, you know, Marker's favorite word, like it depends, but I would say it depends on like what they're trying to achieve, but the reality, especially for B2B SaaS companies today, is that they need to start with like alternative listicles, comparison guides, Sanavis Monday, and also category listicles like project management software and so on and so forth, and then build from there, okay?
But the starting point would be there if we are talking about a new website, like blank canvas, right?
Yeah.
Yeah, it makes sense.
Honestly, it ranges a bit.
I feel like our average cohorts now are certainly skewing more to having a decent set of content.
It used to be more mixed in the past.

Part of that is also us trying to continue to refine our ICP where, you know, we find generally speaking, it's not a hard, fast rule, but generally speaking, Series A plus tends to be a better place for us to start than anything that may be seed stage.
So with that, typically will come more content.
Not always true, but...

If I may say something here, because it's relevant, Kyle, is that, like, you know, I've been to say, like, I developed a process for our content audit service back then, and it's still relevant to this day.
And what we would do is that we would get data from, like, all these data sources.
would have all these data points, like number of keywords, like number of sessions in the...
Last 12 months, last time this piece was updated and so on, and we would assign a weight to each of these variables, okay?
And then we would come up, based on the weight we assigned to each variable, we would come up with a score for each piece of content called, which we, you know, dubbed as an opportunity score, right?
And based on that, we would go and say that, okay, so this page seems that, you it drives leads, it drives revenue or, you know, assisted conversions and whatnot.
Why wouldn't we, you know, update that page first, right?
That's a good and more kind of practical way, I think, to think about prioritizing these opportunities.

If you can, which we never got into the point to do, but if you can semi-automate that and bring all this data together, because it couldn't, it shouldn't be just business data, it should be, you know, like combined with.
Organic search data and whatnot, then you have something that's, you know, really powerful.
Yeah, no, I love that.
It aligns very much with where we're going with our own product.
So how to uncover some of those signals.

And I think the other key complexity related to that, that I've certainly seen at many stops and here as well, is like, how do you have that system of record of what changed in that content over time and like actually understanding it?
Because, I mean, there's just so many variables to consider, right?
And so that should be something that can be done in a more programmatic nature that should be able to lean on the benefits of AI if done right.
So, you know, still building and like bringing that side to life, but that's kind of the vision that we're building towards.
So, yeah, you know, I do think on the refresh side, that is an opportunity that we need to make more repeatable.
And how do we think about it today?
Yeah, we certainly have refresh workflows.
We have different things.
We do to identify opportunities.
I'm not sure, though, we are doing it with enough intent yet.
I would love to get to a point.

I mean, there's an argument to be made of not, like, making this overly complicated and leveraging, you know, what AI can uncover by, you know, doing analysis on different ranking pages and relevant keywords.
Or, you know, there's a million different ways of skin the cat of, like, how would you think about refreshing this content?
And, you know, letting a workflow fed with the right context kind of come up with that.
I think that probably can work.
And that's some version of what we've done a lot.
But I think it needs to be more informed is my kind of hypothesis.
And essentially, like, we get to a point where we have, like, a clear point of view of, like, this is ultimately, like, the experiment we're going to run on this refresh.
And did this generate...
Results or not?
Yes, no.
Okay, move on to the next thing.
You learn this thing, you apply it, you scale it out.
And I think that can apply both to AEO and SEO.
And so I just think we have to figure out a better way of like making that repeatable, easier so than done.

But, you know, I think those are the sorts of things too of like when you can connect the dots of that kind of thinking for our clients, it just helps them see the iteration of like, kind of where we're going.
You know, we don't want to get into the situation where they just feel like we're just continually like producing the same amount of content each week.
We're moving on to different topics, of course, though.
And, you we're looking at some top level metrics.
Like if it feels like that, then they're probably going to be ultimately like underwhelmed at a certain point.
And so there's many ways of addressing that.
But I think, you know, certainly this is one of the ones that continue to bubble up for me.
So, yeah, I love the idea of like figuring out some sort of scoring system and you can always tweak it in a variety of different ways.
But, you know, that.
That makes a lot of sense.
For the record, some tools kind of do that.
Like, for example, ClearScope.
So Profound also has a somewhat kind of similar workflow.

the main issue is that they, for the most part, they don't take into account business data, like leads, assisted conversions, revenue generated, and so on, which you need to make these kinds of decisions.
Now, as you said, like, easier said than done.
But I think that whoever has, brings such a solution to the market wins big time because, you know, like when you combine this data, that's powerful.
Totally, totally.
Well, I know we only have a few minutes left.
Any questions I can help answer for you?
Not really.
I know the business and I know why I'm here in this call.
So I don't have any questions for you at this point.
don't
I can go beyond the 30-minute mark, by the way.
But, yeah, I don't know if you have anything else for me, but I'm good for now.
Okay, cool.
I do have a hard stop in five minutes, but one last question.

Is there a particular example that comes to mind of, say, a key deliverable, if you will, maybe is a key strategic initiative or project or QBR or whatever that was just, like, notably, you realized it was off track and not where it needed to be?
Can you walk me through that, how you identified it, what you did about it?
Yeah, there was an example.
There is an example of, you know, back then we were working with a SaaS company called Toggle.
They're a time tracker.
Now they've expanded to other areas.
But we would publish content or we would update content.
Thank you.
you.
And it wouldn't work, right?
And we would be like, wait a minute, why is this happening?
Like Togo, you know, the website has been, the brand has been around for a while.
The website, you know, is an old website.
It has everything it needs really to perform well, right?
And performance would be like underwhelming, to say the least, right?
Well, the problem was that the website also had, especially the blog, had a lot of underperforming content, which is, you know, kind of connected to what we were discussing just now.
That was essentially, you know, pulling the performance of the whole website down, right?
And unless there was a very serious content audit and, you know, pruning effort and, like, consolidation and whatnot of content, and whatever, like, regardless...

of how many pieces we add, like monthly or scattered efforts we do in terms of like updates and whatnot, you know, when you have a content debt that goes back, I don't know, five, 10 years, I don't think anything can change that.

And so that was a kind of a, you know, an aha moment for me in terms of, okay, it's not just the brand then, right, and the website and all these things that you would like to have to set yourself up for success.

But it's also, how much content do we have, how much of it is actually valuable, and that's, you know, like value in the, like on the web and whatnot, how much of it is obsolete, how much of it can be consolidated into one piece instead of having, you know, four pieces with overlapping information on the same topic and so on.
And so that's one, and, yeah.
Cool, helpful, helpful.
Cool, yeah.
Well, I appreciate you taking the time.
I will connect with Andy, make sure we get another call on the books so you can have a time to chat.
Any questions or anything that comes up, feel free to hit me up via my email, but I appreciate you taking the time.
Great to officially meet after hearing your name earlier in the year or whenever that was.
It all blurs together now.
Thank you.
Thank you, Kyle.
Likewise.
And yeah, I'm looking forward to the next call.
Sounds good.
Thanks, George.
Appreciate it.
Thank you.
Have a nice day.
You too.
Bye.
